case_description:
A 3-year-old female with a history of severe T cell lymphopenia related to [REDACTED] was born at 39-week gestation to a G2P2 mother via repeat Cesarean section at a regional hospital following an uncomplicated pregnancy. She was intubated shortly after birth due to hypoxia and transferred to a tertiary medical center pediatric intensive care unit (PICU); an echocardiogram revealed tetralogy of Fallot with a partially absent and dysplastic pulmonic valve, for which she underwent surgical repair at 2 weeks of age. The absence of an identifiable thymus was noted during this procedure. Her postoperative course was complicated by pulmonary hemorrhage, chylothorax, severe bronchomalacia requiring tracheostomy and intermittent mechanical ventilation, feeding difficulty temporarily requiring G-tube placement, and hypocalcemia.
When immunologically evaluated at 1 month of age, she was noted to have abnormally low T cell receptor excision circle levels (TREC) on newborn metabolic screen, collected on day 3 of life, and later repeated for confirmation (see Table 1). Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), collected on day 1 of life, confirmed a hemizygous chromosomal microdeletion consistent with the suspected syndrome. Initial immunologic studies were delayed due to frequent blood transfusions during management of cardiac complications. Flow cytometry obtained at 2 and 3 months of age demonstrated profound T cell lymphopenia (Table 1). T cell enumeration further demonstrated profoundly decreased total CD3 T cell, CD4 T cell, and CD8 T cell counts for age, but no overt anomaly in T cell subset distribution in either the CD4 or CD8 T cell compartments. Despite initial concerns for the complete (athymic) phenotype due to the severity of T cell lymphopenia, the normal ratio of naïve to memory T cells, the production of naïve T cells (CD45RA+), and the presence of a normal percentage of CD4+ recent thymic emigrants revealed this to be a severe case of the partial (hypoplastic thymus) phenotype (Table 1). Mitogen stimulation testing showed normal proliferative responses to pokeweed mitogen (PWM) and phytohemagglutinin (PHA). Throughout her initial hospitalization for the first 7.5 months of life, the total lymphocyte count ranged from 600 to 2600. Her lymphocyte subsets measured by flow cytometry demonstrated markedly low T, B, and NK cells (see Fig. 1). Only at 28 months of age were her immunoglobulin levels, CD4 lymphocyte, and NK cell counts all normal for age. The total CD3 count remains low for age but continues to increase. In addition to T cell lymphopenia, she demonstrated evidence of humoral immunodeficiency. Immunoglobulin levels obtained at 3 months were markedly low (Table 1). The initial severe hypogammaglobulinemia was also partially attributable to gastrointestinal protein loss via chronic diarrhea, as her albumin was markedly decreased, consistent with protein-losing enteropathy. Her total IgG level improved but did not normalize despite eventual resolution of the diarrhea and other signs of enteropathy, suggesting that she also had underlying primary hypogammaglobulinemia. She received immunoglobulin replacement through age 19 months. Her IgG level continued to spontaneously increase after discontinuation of replacement therapy.
During the initial hospitalization, she was administered prophylaxis for Mycobacterium avium complex, Pneumocystis pneumonia, and invasive candidiasis. Mycobacterium prophylaxis was discontinued prior to discharge due to clinical improvement. Other prophylaxis was continued until after she achieved immune reconstitution. She has now received both inactivated and live vaccines, without complication. Her specific antibody levels measured 10 months after her last dose of IVIG were protective for tetanus, diphtheria, and 18 of 22 measured Streptococcus pneumoniae serotypes. Her initial hospital course was complicated by intermittent febrile episodes; workup for underlying infection was unrevealing except for separate instances of bacterial tracheitis and bacterial urinary tract infection which were appropriately treated. Between her initial hospital discharge and first birthday, she was hospitalized multiple times for suspected upper and lower respiratory tract infections. Bacterial tracheitis was identified on one occasion, but no causative organisms were identified during the other hospital admissions. To date, she has continued to have viral respiratory infections at a normal frequency for age, with intermittent brief hospital observation. She was infected with SARS-CoV-2 at ages 25 months and 42 months. She developed a fever without other symptoms with the initial SARS-CoV-2 infection and was asymptomatic with the second infection. She has had no opportunistic infections. Written parental consent and a waiver from the institutional IRB were obtained regarding publishing this data.

case_description: A 9-month-old male is brought to clinic by his parents for evaluation of disproportionate short stature. On examination, his trunk length is appropriate for age but his arms and legs are notably short, particularly in the proximal segments (humerus and femur). His head circumference is above the 97th percentile with frontal bossing and a depressed nasal bridge. He has hyperextensibility of most joints except at the elbows, where extension is limited. His hands show a "trident" configuration. Motor milestones are mildly delayed. Family history is negative; both parents are of average height.

A 7-year-old boy is referred for evaluation of multiple skin lesions. On examination, he has 11 cafe-au-lait macules ranging from 1 to 4 cm in diameter, axillary and inguinal freckling, and three soft cutaneous nodules on the trunk. Slit-lamp examination identifies six pigmented iris hamartomas bilaterally. He has mild learning difficulties and ADHD. Height is at the 25th percentile; head circumference is at the 90th percentile. His mother has similar skin findings and has had a benign optic nerve tumor.

case_description: A 3-year-old girl is referred for evaluation of developmental delay and a heart murmur. Her parents describe her as unusually friendly and verbose with strangers, with strong language skills but poor visuospatial abilities. Examination shows a small upturned nose, full cheeks, wide mouth with full lips, small widely-spaced teeth, and a stellate iris pattern. A loud systolic murmur is heard at the upper right sternal border. Echocardiogram demonstrates supravalvular aortic stenosis. Serum calcium is 11.8 mg/dL. Family history is unremarkable.

case_description: A 6-month-old infant presents with new-onset infantile spasms. EEG shows hypsarrhythmia. Examination reveals three hypopigmented oval macules on the trunk (best visualized under Wood's lamp), each 2-3 cm in length and resembling an ash leaf. Ophthalmologic examination identifies multiple retinal hamartomas bilaterally. Brain MRI shows multiple cortical tubers and subependymal nodules. Echocardiogram demonstrates two cardiac masses consistent with rhabdomyomas. The father has been treated for a renal mass and has facial papules in a butterfly distribution.

case_description: A 14-month-old African American child is brought to the emergency department with painful swelling of both hands and feet of 2 days' duration. He has had three previous similar episodes. His mother reports that he has been increasingly fussy and pale. On examination, both hands and the dorsa of the feet are swollen, warm, and tender. Hemoglobin is 7.2 g/dL with reticulocytosis. Peripheral smear shows numerous elongated, crescent-shaped red blood cells along with target cells. Both parents are healthy of West African descent.

case_description: A 3-week-old infant is referred after an abnormal newborn screening result. The infant appears clinically well, with normal growth parameters and unremarkable examination. Plasma phenylalanine concentration is markedly elevated at 1,650 umol/L (normal <100). Tyrosine is low. Urine has a faintly musty odor. Repeat testing on a separate sample confirms the elevation. The parents are non-consanguineous and have no family history of metabolic disorders.

case_description: A 48-year-old man of Northern European ancestry presents with progressive fatigue, joint pain affecting the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally, and reduced libido. On examination, his skin has a bronze hue most prominent on sun-exposed areas. Liver edge is palpable 3 cm below the costal margin. Laboratory studies show fasting glucose of 156 mg/dL, AST 78, ALT 95, ferritin 2,100 ng/mL, and transferrin saturation of 89%. A maternal uncle died of liver cirrhosis without alcohol use.

case_description: A 4-month-old boy is admitted for evaluation of recurrent infections and persistent eczema. He has had two episodes of otitis media, one episode of pneumococcal sepsis, and chronic atopic-appearing dermatitis since age 2 months. His mother has noticed bruising with minimal trauma and frequent epistaxis. Laboratory studies show a platelet count of 38,000/uL with markedly reduced mean platelet volume (4.2 fL). Serum IgM is low; IgA is elevated. A maternal uncle died in infancy of bleeding complications and infections.

case_description: A 19-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of facial and extremity swelling, each lasting 2-4 days. The swelling is non-pruritic, non-pitting, and not associated with urticaria. She has had two prior episodes of severe abdominal pain with vomiting requiring hospitalization, during which CT scans showed bowel wall edema without other pathology. Antihistamines and corticosteroids have provided no benefit during attacks. Episodes are sometimes precipitated by minor trauma or dental procedures. Her mother and one of two sisters have similar attacks. C4 complement level is markedly low between attacks.

case_description: A 65-year-old man presents with a 5-day history of productive cough, fever, chills, and shortness of breath. He reports yellow sputum and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. Temperature is 38.8°C, heart rate 104 bpm, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 92% on room air. Lung examination reveals crackles over the right lower lobe. Chest radiograph demonstrates a focal right lower lobe infiltrate.

case_description: A 68-year-old man with a 45-pack-year smoking history presents with worsening shortness of breath and chronic productive cough over several years. He reports increasing difficulty climbing stairs and frequent winter bronchitis episodes. Oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. Pulmonary examination reveals prolonged expiration and diffuse wheezing. Spirometry demonstrates an FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.58 with incomplete reversibility after bronchodilator administration.

case_description: A 57-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician for a routine annual examination. She reports occasional headaches over the past several months but denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or dizziness. She has a sedentary lifestyle and consumes a high-sodium diet. BMI is 31 kg/m². Blood pressure measured on three separate visits ranges from 152/94 mmHg to 158/96 mmHg. Laboratory studies including fasting glucose, serum creatinine, and lipid profile are within normal limits.
